bibliographic_data.title: Towards Understanding Quality Challenges of the Federated Learning for Neural Networks: A First Look from the Lens of Robustness
bibliographic_data.abstract: Federated learning (FL) is a distributed learning paradigm that preserves users’ data privacy while leveraging the entire dataset of all participants. In FL, multiple models are trained independently on clients and aggregated centrally to update a global model in an iterative process. Although this approach is excellent at preserving privacy, FL still suffers from quality issues such as attacks or byzantine faults. Recent attempts have been made to address such quality challenges on the robust aggregation techniques for FL. However, the effectiveness of state-of-the-art (SOTA) robust FL techniques is still unclear and lacks a comprehensive study. Therefore, to better understand the current quality status and challenges of these SOTA FL techniques in the presence of attacks and faults, we perform a large-scale empirical study to investigate the SOTA FL’s quality from multiple angles of attacks, simulated faults (via mutation operators), and aggregation (defense) methods. In particular, we study FL's performance on image classification tasks and use Deep Neural Networks as our model type. Furthermore, we perform our study on two generic image datasets and one real-world federated medical image dataset. We also systematically investigate the effect of the proportion of affected clients and the dataset distribution factors on the robustness of FL.

core_content.novelty_claim: The study performs a large-scale empirical analysis to understand the effectiveness of state-of-the-art (SOTA) robust federated learning techniques in the presence of attacks and faults.
core_content.key_methodology: The authors conduct experiments on four FL attacks and four DL mutation operators, evaluating the impact of these attacks/faults on image classification tasks using Deep Neural Networks. They also study the effect of the proportion of affected clients and dataset distribution factors on the robustness of federated learning.
outcomes: Most mutators have a negligible effect on the final model in generic datasets, but one is effective in the medical dataset.
Model poisoning attacks are more effective than data poisoning attacks.
Choosing the most robust FL aggregator depends on the attacks and datasets.
A simple ensemble of aggregators achieves a more robust solution than any single aggregator and is the best choice in 75% of the cases.
core_content.full_text_markdown: The study investigates the quality challenges of federated learning (FL) for neural networks, particularly focusing on robustness. FL is a distributed learning paradigm that preserves user data privacy while leveraging the entire dataset from all participants. The authors perform an extensive empirical analysis to understand the effectiveness of state-of-the-art (SOTA) robust FL techniques in the presence of attacks and faults. They conduct experiments on four FL attacks and four DL mutation operators, evaluating their impact on image classification tasks using Deep Neural Networks. The study also examines the effect of the proportion of affected clients and dataset distribution factors on the robustness of federated learning. Key findings include that most mutators have a negligible effect in generic datasets but are more impactful in medical datasets. Model poisoning attacks are found to be more effective than data poisoning attacks, and choosing the right aggregator depends on both the type of attack and the dataset. The study concludes that an ensemble of aggregators is more robust and performs best in 75% of cases.
